home. Many left with the sincere desire to return and field
many will return, yet they all, for varying lengths of time,
left to feel the outer world and to test life and educate
themselves, in part, by being away from the home and
family structure. Maybe this runs counter to the idea
that to populate the countryside we must keep the
children around. Maybe there is a leaving and returning, a
cycling of energy that must naturally occur.

It is in the leaving that we know what we miss. Craig
Fernholz now lives in the Uptown area in Minneapolis.
More from Katie: “Alaska taught me the value of stability.
“One thing I do miss, living here, is the stars. Coming to the
Alaska was exhausting, cut-throat, and competitive.
Cities—well, I finally saw my first shooting star last night
Everything in Alaska is gone tomorrow; there is no stability,

There are those phrases again: “I longed for . . . and I
learned.” Is it through longing that we do finally learn?
And how do you “long for” anything without leaving it? Inga Haugen is attending college at Concordia in
